securityPolicy.note: Anthropic's monorepo policy applies; private vulnerability reporting via Security tab.

signedReleases.note: npm package published with provenance attestations.

codeReview.note: PRs go through the modelcontextprotocol org; public review history.

secretsHandling.note: Server itself stores no secrets. If your agent reads a file containing a secret, that secret enters the LLM context — that's your prompt's problem, not the server's.

sandboxModel.note: Runs as subprocess of the MCP host. The path allow-list is enforced inside the server process itself.

filesystemAccess.note: Hard-scoped at server launch via the allow-list paths. The server refuses operations on paths outside the list.

dataResidency.note: Files stay on your machine. Only the agent's reads enter the LLM context (over whatever transport you've configured).

defaultPermissions.note: By default the server exposes read AND write AND delete tools. You'd need a fork or a wrapper to ship a read-only mode.

destructiveTools.note: MCP host (e.g. Claude Desktop) is responsible for approval prompts. The server doesn't gate destructive calls itself.

promptInjectionExposure.note: File contents the agent reads can contain hostile instructions. Treat all read output as untrusted text — particularly README.md, code comments, and any data file under attacker influence.

supplyChainSurface.note: Small JS dep tree. Direct deps are MCP SDK, glob matcher, and a few Node-native modules.

depCveSurface.note: No active CVEs at last scan.

rateLimitRisk.note: No rate limits — purely local I/O.

maintainerType.note: Maintained by Anthropic and the modelcontextprotocol working group.

verdictScope: Local file operations on a tightly-scoped allow-list of directories.
verdictNote: Use it. Scope the allow-list to one repo subfolder, not $HOME. For unsupervised agents, drop write tools entirely (fork or wrap); for supervised agents, configure your host to gate destructive calls with explicit approval.
